--- SOURCE 1: Rising Expenses and Food Insecurity: An American Reality ---
URL: https://www.bread.org/article/rising-expenses-and-food-insecurity-an-american-reality/

SUMMARY:
Inflation boosts prices for consumers, making it harder to afford nutritious food. Even the cost of basic products, such as cereals and bakery



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 2: How inflation is hurting the diets of low-income Americans ---
URL: https://www.northwell.edu/news/the-latest/inflation-and-american-diets

SUMMARY:
Higher food prices hurt those with lower incomes the most, limiting access to healthy food and impacting long-term health.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 3: Food Insecurity in the US and Inflation ---
URL: https://www.aei.org/center-on-opportunity-and-social-mobility/food-insecurity-in-the-us-and-inflation/

SUMMARY:
Food inflation has decelerated in 2023, with the 12-month percentage change in CPI for food at home declining to 3.7 percent in September 2023

How do Economic Growth and Food Inflation Affect Food Insecurity?
-----------------------------------------------------------------

By [Christian Bogmans,](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/Publications-By-Author?author=Christian+Bogmans&name=Christian%20Bogmans)[Andrea Pescatori,](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/Publications-By-Author?author=Andrea+Pescatori&name=Andrea%20Pescatori)[Ervin Prifti](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/Publications-By-Author?author=Ervin+Prifti&name=Ervin%20Prifti)

September 6, 2024

Preview Citation
----------------

![Image 16: copy icon](https://www.imf.org/-/media/Images/IMF/Icons-generic/Publications/Issues/copy.ashx) Copy 
Format: Chicago

Christian Bogmans, Andrea Pescatori, and Ervin Prifti. _How do Economic Growth and Food Inflation Affect Food Insecurity?_, (USA: International Monetary Fund, 2024) accessed June 29, 2025

**Disclaimer:** IMF Working Papers describe research in progress by the author(s) and are published to elicit comments and to encourage debate. The views expressed in IMF Working Papers are those of the author(s) and do not necessarily represent the views of the IMF, its Executive Board, or IMF management.

### Summary

 During the global recession of 2020 food insecurity increased substantially in many countries around the world. Fortunately, the surge in food insecurity quickly came to a halt as the world economy returned to its positive growth path, despite double-digit domestic food inflation in most countries. To shed light on the relative importance of income growth and food inflation in driving food insecurity, we employ a heterogeneous-agent model with income inequality, complemented by novel cross-country data for the period 2001-2021. We use external instruments (changes in commodity terms-of-trade, external economic growth, and harvest shocks) to isolate exogenous variation in domestic income growth and ood inflation. Our findings suggest that income growth is the dominant driver of annual variations in food insecurity, while food price inflation plays a somewhat smaller role, aligning with our model predictions. 

**Subject:**[Food prices,](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/Publications-By-Subject?subject=Food%20prices)[Food security,](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/Publications-By-Subject?subject=Food%20security)[Income,](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/Publications-By-Subject?subject=Income)[Income inequality,](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/Publications-By-Subject?subject=Income%20inequality)[Inflation,](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/Publications-By-Subject?subject=Inflation)[National accounts,](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/Publications-By-Subject?subject=National%20accounts)[Poverty,](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/Publications-By-Subject?subject=Poverty)[Price elasticity,](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/Publications-By-Subject?subject=Price%20elasticity)[Prices](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/Publications-By-Subject?subject=Prices)

**Keywords:**Diet., Food inflation, Food insecurity, Food prices, Food security, Global, Inclusive growth, Income, Income inequality, Inequality, Inflation, Price elasticity

--- SOURCE 5: Food Security | Food Insecurity Statistics & Solutions ---
URL: https://www.worldbank.org/en/topic/agriculture/brief/food-security-update

SUMMARY:
*   Through the $50 million Emergency Food Security Response project, 329,000 smallholder farmers in Central Africa Republic have received seeds, farming tools and training in agricultural and post-harvest techniques to boost crop production and become more resilient to climate and conflict risks. *   The $15 million Guinea Bissau Emergency Food Security Project is helping increase agriculture production and access to food to vulnerable families. *   A $125 million project in Jordan aims to strengthen the development the agriculture sector by enhancing its climate resilience, increasing competitiveness and inclusion, and ensuring medium- to long-term food security.

FULL CONTENT:
Published Time: Wed, 30 Jul 2025 23:42:22 GMT

Food Security | Food Insecurity Statistics & Solutions

URL: https://www.zerohedge.com/s3/files/inline-images/BP02-Countries-with-the-Highest.jpg?itok=NWjAtaal
DESCRIPTION: The image shows a chart illustrating the impact of inflation on food access, highlighting how rising food inflation, driven partly by declining potash crop yields, affects vulnerable populations in countries such as Argentina, Palestine, Zimbabwe, and Turkey.

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://econofact.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/07/Food-Inflation-social.png
DESCRIPTION: A graph displaying the annual inflation rates of food prices from January 2020 to July 2022, with distinct lines representing the USA, Eurozone, and Emerging Markets, highlighting the trends and fluctuations over time.

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://www.kzndard.gov.za/images/Documents/DARD_Projects/Food-Security-and-the-impact-of-Food-Inflation.jpg
DESCRIPTION: None

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://samples.freshessays.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/Picture1-19.png
DESCRIPTION: A line graph illustrates how food inflation, non-food inflation, and CPI inflation have increased over time in sub-Saharan Africa, with food inflation surpassing the other two metrics, highlighting the rising impact of food prices on consumer access.

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://www.openaccessgovernment.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/iStock-1428386188-scaled.jpg
DESCRIPTION: A person shopping for groceries, including milk and bread, in a supermarket aisle with shelves stocked with various food products, possibly highlighting how inflation affects food access.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Inflation drives up food prices, making it increasingly difficult for low-income families to afford essential nutritious foods, exacerbating food insecurity.
High unemployment rates reduce household incomes, limiting purchasing power and access to food, particularly healthy options, leading to increased food insecurity.
Economic barriers hinder access to nutritious food, as rising costs disproportionately affect low-income populations, impacting their overall health and well-being.
Food deserts limit access to affordable and nutritious food, often leaving low-income communities reliant on expensive, unhealthy food options, exacerbating dietary-related health issues.
